## [06:03 - 09:05]

important to understand is that the tennis court is a rectangle. The distance cross court is greater than the distance down the line. The net is lower in the middle than it is on than it is by the single sidelines. And the reason that's important is because based on the dimensions of the tennis court not all shots are created equal. And this is something a lot of rec players don't necessarily think about. The cross court shot is safer. There is more court to hit into. You can hit a little bit longer. The ball is gonna be in versus going down the line and the net is lower. So the net is less of an obstacle going cross court than it is if you hit down the line. So again the key concept there not all shots created equal cross court is safer than down the line. So if you're making a lot of errors down the line one of the reasons is you know there's not as much court to work with and you've got a higher obstacle with the net. Now the second concept is something called directionals. And this is a term coined by Paul Wardlaw to describe high percentage and low percentage shots because based on how the ball is coming into you based on how your opponent and how it's actually traveling towards you some shots are going to be easier to hit than other shots. And before we go any further we've got this player icon here and you know the circle is the player that sticks the side they're hitting on. This is a forehand for a righty, a backhand for a righty. This line would travel straight down the court through the plane of the player. And Wardlaw talked about something called inside and outside ground strokes. And the key concept is that the inside ground strokes are better. They're preferable to the outside ground strokes. And the reason is because the inside ground strokes are easier to control. In other words we can do more with those inside ground strokes. So let's specifically define what an outside ground stroke is, what an inside ground stroke is. We'll start with an outside ground stroke. And simply put an outside ground stroke is where the ball is going to cross the plane of your body and be moving away from you when you hit it. So again those ones are a little tougher to control than those inside ground strokes. We'll get to that in a second. My foam ball is rolling away from me. So the last thing we want to do is talk about court positioning. Where do you want to stand on the court during a rally? And I've got three points up here, three points down here. These are your court positioning points. Now, the key with court positioning is that you want to position yourself in between the max angles that your opponent can generate based on where they are hitting the ball on the tennis court. So in other words, if you've got your opponent and he's smack dab in the middle of the court, the maximum angles he can generate are going to be equal more or less on both end of the court. And because of that, you want to position yourself in the middle of the court as well. Again, that'll put you right in the middle of those max angles. But if we've got your opponent on one side or the other, let's say that he's hitting a forehand over here, suddenly the angles to this area of the court aren't as great. He can't pull you off the court as much, but over here, he has a little bit better of an angle to work with. So it might look something like this. And because of that, now you actually want to position yourself about right here on this positioning point. That will put you in between the max angles that your opponent can generate. Now, it's also worth noting that if you get in a cross court rally like this, like we just talked about in that previous segment, the down the line isn't quite as likely simply because it's a lower percentage shot. ## [18:11 - 21:14]

typical strength for a rec player. You've got the weaknesses. Backhand tends to be the weaker side for a rec player. Opportunities is what your opponent is weak at.
